explanations.1: Linear momentum is defined as the product of mass in kilograms and velocity in meters per second.
explanations.2: Newton’s second law states that the net external force equals the change in momentum divided by the time over which it changes.
explanations.3: Using the formula $p = mv$, the momentum is calculated as $110\text{ kg} \times 8.0\text{ m/s} = 880\text{ kg}\cdot\text{m/s}$.
explanations.4: Impulse is the name given to the quantity $F_{net}\Delta t$, which also equals the change in momentum.
explanations.5: By increasing the collision time, the average force required to achieve the same change in momentum is decreased.
explanations.6: The integral of force over time (the area under the F-t curve) is equal to the change in momentum, or impulse.
explanations.7: The change in momentum is $5\text{ kg}\cdot\text{m/s}$ ($0.5\text{ kg} \times 10\text{ m/s}$), and force is $\Delta p / \Delta t$, which is $5 / 0.05 = 100\text{ N}$.
explanations.8: Momentum is conserved in an isolated system, which is defined as one where the net external force is zero.
explanations.9: Backward scattering implies the electron hit something very small and dense within the proton, providing evidence for quarks.
explanations.10: Momentum is conserved along any dimension where the net external force is zero; gravity acts as an external force in the vertical direction.
explanations.11: An elastic collision is specifically defined as one that conserves the sum of the kinetic energies of the objects in the system.
explanations.12: Macroscopic collisions are rarely perfectly elastic, but hard objects on frictionless surfaces like steel on ice come very close.
explanations.13: Conservation of momentum: $(0.5 \times 4) + 0 = (0.5 \times -3) + (3.5 \times v')$, which simplifies to $2 = -1.5 + 3.5v'$, so $v' = 1.00\text{ m/s}$.
explanations.14: In a one-dimensional elastic collision of equal masses where one is at rest, the objects swap velocities to conserve both momentum and kinetic energy.
explanations.15: An inelastic collision is defined by a change in the system's total internal kinetic energy, usually lost to heat or sound.
explanations.16: A perfectly inelastic collision is one where the objects stick together, resulting in the maximum possible loss of internal kinetic energy.
explanations.17: $m_1v_1 = (m_1 + m_2)v'$, so $(0.15 \times 35) = (70.15)v'$, giving $v' \approx 0.0748\text{ m/s}$, rounded to $0.075\text{ m/s}$.
explanations.18: Inelastic collisions can involve an increase in kinetic energy if internal potential energy is converted during the event.
explanations.19: A heavier racquet has more mass and thus more momentum, which is the key factor in determining the ball's outgoing velocity.
explanations.20: Two-dimensional problems are treated as two independent one-dimensional problems by resolving vectors into components along the x and y axes.
explanations.21: Point masses are structureless particles used in simplified models to avoid the complexities of rotation during collisions.
explanations.22: For elastic collisions of equal masses where one is initially at rest, the scattering angles $\theta_1 - \theta_2$ typically sum to $90$ degrees.
explanations.23: Since initial momentum in the y-direction was zero, the final y-momenta must sum to zero to satisfy conservation laws.
explanations.24: Rockets work by forcefully ejecting gas in one direction, which creates an equal and opposite reaction force (thrust) on the rocket.
explanations.25: A rocket’s acceleration is $a = \frac{v_e}{m}\frac{\Delta m}{\Delta t} - g$; atmospheric pressure is not a primary factor and thrust is actually higher in a vacuum.
explanations.26: Since fuel makes up most of a rocket's mass, burning it significantly reduces the denominator ($m$) in the acceleration equation.
explanations.27: Each stage is discarded after use so that the remaining stages don't have to accelerate the dead weight of empty tanks.
explanations.28: Momentum is conserved when the net external force is zero; friction is an external force that can change a system's momentum.
explanations.30: Since $\Delta p = m\Delta v$, the equation becomes $F = m(\Delta v / \Delta t)$, and $\Delta v / \Delta t$ is acceleration ($a$).
